---
title: "Ch 5 — Regular Polyhedra and Similar Solids"
subtitle: "Why only five Platonic solids exist — and how volume scales as the cube of length"
lesson-id: "kg2.regular-polyhedra"
tier: "kg2"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book II (Stereometry)"
kiselev-chapter: 5
kiselev-chapter-title: "Regular polyhedra and similarity"
kiselev-sections: "Stereometry §§491–530 (regular polyhedra, Euler's formula, similar solids)"
prerequisites: [kg2.polyhedra, kg2.sphere]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research, ai-ml]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Are prism, pyramid, sphere formulas in your active memory? See
   [`kg2.polyhedra`](../02-polyhedra/index.qmd) and
   [`kg2.sphere`](../04-sphere/index.qmd).
2. Can you state the *similarity-area-cube* rule from Planimetry's
   similarity chapter?
3. Are you comfortable with the names *cube*, *tetrahedron*,
   *octahedron* — even if you've never met *dodecahedron* or
   *icosahedron*?
4. Can you compute the interior angle of a regular pentagon
   ($108°$)?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

Five solids — and **only** five — have the property that every face
is the same regular polygon, and the same number of faces meet at
every vertex. Drag through them.

```{ojs}
viewof solid = Inputs.radio(
  ["tetrahedron (4)", "cube (6)", "octahedron (8)", "dodecahedron (12)", "icosahedron (20)"],
  {value: "cube (6)", label: "Platonic solid (faces):"}
)
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 380, h = 320, padX = 30, padY = 30;
  const cx = w / 2, cy = h / 2;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  // simple isometric-ish 2D projections of each Platonic solid (schematic)
  const project = (x, y, z) => [cx + x + 0.45 * y, cy - z - 0.35 * y];

  const drawCube = () => {
    const s = 60;
    const V = [
      [-s,-s,-s],[s,-s,-s],[s,s,-s],[-s,s,-s],
      [-s,-s,s],[s,-s,s],[s,s,s],[-s,s,s]
    ].map(p => project(...p));
    const E = [[0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,0],[4,5],[5,6],[6,7],[7,4],[0,4],[1,5],[2,6],[3,7]];
    for (const [a,b] of E) svg.append("line")
      .attr("x1", V[a][0]).attr("y1", V[a][1]).attr("x2", V[b][0]).attr("y2", V[b][1])
      .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
  };

  const drawTet = () => {
    const s = 80;
    const V = [[0,0,s],[s,0,-s/2],[-s/2,s,-s/2],[-s/2,-s,-s/2]].map(p => project(...p));
    const E = [[0,1],[0,2],[0,3],[1,2],[2,3],[3,1]];
    for (const [a,b] of E) svg.append("line")
      .attr("x1", V[a][0]).attr("y1", V[a][1]).attr("x2", V[b][0]).attr("y2", V[b][1])
      .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
  };

  const drawOct = () => {
    const s = 80;
    const V = [[s,0,0],[-s,0,0],[0,s,0],[0,-s,0],[0,0,s],[0,0,-s]].map(p => project(...p));
    const E = [[0,2],[2,1],[1,3],[3,0],[0,4],[1,4],[2,4],[3,4],[0,5],[1,5],[2,5],[3,5]];
    for (const [a,b] of E) svg.append("line")
      .attr("x1", V[a][0]).attr("y1", V[a][1]).attr("x2", V[b][0]).attr("y2", V[b][1])
      .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
  };

  // dodecahedron and icosahedron — schematic outlines (not full vertex networks)
  const drawDodec = () => {
    // simplified: draw a regular pentagon front + back + connectors
    const r = 70;
    const pent = (cx0, cy0, sign) => {
      const pts = [];
      for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        const ang = -Math.PI / 2 + 2 * Math.PI * i / 5;
        pts.push([cx0 + r * Math.cos(ang), cy0 + sign * r * Math.sin(ang)]);
      }
      return pts;
    };
    const front = pent(cx, cy, 1);
    svg.append("polygon").attr("points", front.map(p => `${p[0]},${p[1]}`).join(" "))
      .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", cx).attr("y", cy + r + 16).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#666").text("(schematic — full dodecahedron has 12 pentagons)");
  };

  const drawIcos = () => {
    // schematic: triangulated pentagonal antiprism approximation
    const r = 70;
    const cx0 = cx, cy0 = cy;
    for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
      const a1 = -Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*i/5;
      const a2 = -Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*(i+1)/5;
      const x1 = cx0 + r * Math.cos(a1), y1 = cy0 + r * Math.sin(a1);
      const x2 = cx0 + r * Math.cos(a2), y2 = cy0 + r * Math.sin(a2);
      svg.append("line").attr("x1", x1).attr("y1", y1).attr("x2", x2).attr("y2", y2)
        .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
      svg.append("line").attr("x1", x1).attr("y1", y1).attr("x2", cx0).attr("y2", cy0)
        .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 0.8);
    }
    svg.append("text").attr("x", cx).attr("y", cy + r + 16).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#666").text("(schematic — full icosahedron has 20 triangles)");
  };

  if (solid.startsWith("cube")) drawCube();
  else if (solid.startsWith("tet")) drawTet();
  else if (solid.startsWith("oct")) drawOct();
  else if (solid.startsWith("dodec")) drawDodec();
  else drawIcos();

  // V, E, F counts per Platonic solid
  const VEF = {
    "tetrahedron (4)":  [4, 6, 4],
    "cube (6)":         [8, 12, 6],
    "octahedron (8)":   [6, 12, 8],
    "dodecahedron (12)":[20, 30, 12],
    "icosahedron (20)": [12, 30, 20]
  };
  const [V, E, F] = VEF[solid];
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
    .attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`${solid}:  V=${V}, E=${E}, F=${F}.   V−E+F = ${V - E + F}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

Five solids: **tetrahedron** (4 triangles), **cube** (6 squares),
**octahedron** (8 triangles), **dodecahedron** (12 pentagons),
**icosahedron** (20 triangles). For every one of them,
$V - E + F = 2$. *Euler's formula.*

## Wonder

1. Why exactly **five** regular polyhedra? Could there be a sixth?
2. The five Platonic solids have $V - E + F = 2$. Is this special
   to them, or true for *any* convex polyhedron?
3. Two solids similar with linear ratio $k$. The volumes are in
   ratio $k^3$, which everyone knows. Why $k^3$ and not, say, $k^2$
   or $k^{2.5}$?
4. The square-cube law: a giant ant cannot exist. What does that
   have to do with the geometry of similar solids?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) The proof of the **5-Platonic-solids theorem** — that no sixth
exists — is one of the most elegant in geometry; it falls out of
$V - E + F = 2$ and the angle constraint at each vertex. (2)
**Euler's formula holds for every convex polyhedron** — and indeed
for every polyhedron that is topologically a sphere. (3) Volume is
length cubed because volume is a triple integral — three
length-dimensions, one each for $x, y, z$. (4) Doubling a creature's
size multiplies its mass (∝ volume) by $8$ but its leg-cross-section
(∝ area) by only $4$. The legs cannot bear the doubled weight.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Definition of regular polyhedron

A **regular polyhedron** is a convex polyhedron such that:
(a) every face is the same regular polygon;
(b) the same number of faces meet at every vertex.
Show that this also forces all *dihedral angles* to be equal and
all *edges* to be the same length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By (a), all face edges have the same length (regular faces of the
same shape). Since adjacent faces share an edge, all edges of the
polyhedron have the same length.

For dihedral angles: at any edge, two faces meet. By (a) and the
constancy of vertex configuration, the local geometry near every
edge is *congruent* to the local geometry near every other edge —
hence the dihedral angle is the same. **QED.**
*(Stereometry §492.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Euler's formula

Show that for any **convex** polyhedron with $V$ vertices, $E$
edges, $F$ faces,
$$V - E + F = 2.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
*Cauchy's proof.* Remove one face, flatten the remaining shell onto
the plane (no creasing — pretend it's elastic). The flattened
result is a *connected planar graph* on $V$ vertices, $E$ edges,
and $F - 1$ "regions" (the missing face becomes the outside).
Apply Euler's formula for *planar graphs*: $V - E + (F - 1) = 1$,
i.e. $V - E + F = 2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Cauchy's proof, sketched* (Stereometry §494):

Remove one face from the polyhedron, leaving a shell with a hole.
Stretch the shell flat onto the plane — the hole becomes the
infinite region. The result is a *connected planar graph* with
$V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F - 1$ bounded regions plus one
infinite region (= $F$ total regions, but the infinite one is the
"removed" face we had taken away).

For any connected planar graph, **Euler's formula for planar
graphs** asserts $V - E + F_{\text{regions}} = 2$. Since
$F_{\text{regions}} = F$ here (counting the infinite region in
place of the removed face), we get $V - E + F = 2$.

The planar-graph version of Euler's formula is proved by induction:
on a single edge, $2 - 1 + 1 = 2$. Adding an edge that creates a
new region preserves $V - E + F$ (loses 1 from $E$, gains 1 from
$F$). Adding an edge that connects to a new vertex preserves it
too (gains 1 from $V$, gains 1 from $E$, no new region). **QED.**
*(Stereometry §494 — the celebrated **Euler-Cauchy formula**.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 — Only five regular polyhedra

Suppose every face is a regular $p$-gon and exactly $q$ faces meet
at each vertex. Show that $(p, q)$ must be one of the five pairs:
$(3, 3), (4, 3), (3, 4), (5, 3), (3, 5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
*Vertex-angle constraint:* the $q$ face-angles meeting at a vertex
have measures summing to *less than* $360°$ (since the polyhedron
is convex). Each face-angle is the interior angle of a regular
$p$-gon, $(p - 2) \cdot 180° / p$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At each vertex, $q$ regular $p$-gon corners meet, total angle
$q \cdot \dfrac{(p - 2) \cdot 180°}{p} < 360°$ (convexity).
Simplify: $q (p - 2) < 2p$, i.e. $\dfrac{1}{p} + \dfrac{1}{q} >
\dfrac{1}{2}$.

Both $p \ge 3$ and $q \ge 3$ (need at least 3 sides per face, at
least 3 faces per vertex). Solutions:

| $(p, q)$ | $\tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q}$ | Solid |
|----------|------|-------|
| $(3, 3)$ | $\tfrac{2}{3} > 1/2$ | Tetrahedron |
| $(3, 4)$ | $\tfrac{7}{12}$ | Octahedron |
| $(3, 5)$ | $\tfrac{8}{15}$ | Icosahedron |
| $(4, 3)$ | $\tfrac{7}{12}$ | Cube |
| $(5, 3)$ | $\tfrac{8}{15}$ | Dodecahedron |
| $(3, 6)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ — **fails** | (degenerate flat tiling) |
| $(4, 4)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ — fails | (square tiling) |
| $(6, 3)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ — fails | (hex tiling) |

Five — no more. **QED.** *(Stereometry §493 — *the Platonic
classification*. Plato's *Timaeus* names them; Euclid's *Elements*
proves the count in Book XIII.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 — Volume of similar solids scales as $k^3$

Two similar solids have linear ratio $k$. Show that their volumes
are in ratio $k^3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For *cubes*, scaling each side by $k$ multiplies volume by $k^3$.
For arbitrary solids, decompose into small cubes and take a limit
(a 3D analogue of the polygon-into-triangles argument from Ch 9 of
Planimetry).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Cube case.** Cube of side $s$: volume $s^3$. Cube scaled by $k$:
volume $(ks)^3 = k^3 s^3$.

**General case.** Approximate the solid by a union of small cubes
of side $\epsilon$. Volume of the union $\approx N \epsilon^3$
where $N$ is the cube count. The scaled solid is approximated by
the same union of cubes, each scaled to side $k\epsilon$ — total
volume $\approx N (k\epsilon)^3 = k^3 N \epsilon^3$. The ratio is
$k^3$ for every $\epsilon$; in the limit $\epsilon \to 0$, the
ratio remains $k^3$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §502 — *the cube-of-
linear-ratio scaling*.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 — Surface area scales as $k^2$

Show that the surface area of similar solids scales as $k^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The same argument as for area in 2D (Ch 7 of Planimetry): each
face scales by $k^2$ in area, and surface is the sum of face
areas. **QED.**
</details>

### Problem 6 — Volumes of the Platonic solids

Compute the volumes of all five Platonic solids of edge length $1$,
in closed form.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
| Solid | Volume (edge $= 1$) |
|-------|---------------------|
| Tetrahedron | $\dfrac{\sqrt 2}{12} \approx 0.1178$ |
| Cube | $1$ |
| Octahedron | $\dfrac{\sqrt 2}{3} \approx 0.4714$ |
| Dodecahedron | $\dfrac{15 + 7\sqrt 5}{4} \approx 7.6631$ |
| Icosahedron | $\dfrac{5(3 + \sqrt 5)}{12} \approx 2.1817$ |

The tetrahedron formula was derived in Ch 2 P1. The cube is
trivial. Octahedron: think of it as two square pyramids glued at
their square bases — each pyramid has square base of side $1$ and
height $\sqrt{2}/2$, giving volume $\sqrt 2 / 6$ each, total
$\sqrt 2 / 3$. The dodecahedron and icosahedron involve the golden
ratio $\varphi = (1 + \sqrt 5)/2$ and require careful coordinate
computation. *(Stereometry §498 — see Givental for the dodecahedron
and icosahedron derivations.)*
</details>

### Problem 7 — Square-cube law

A creature is scaled up uniformly by factor $k$. Mass scales by
$k^3$ (volume). Bone cross-section scales by $k^2$ (area). Stress
on bone (force per area) scales by what?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Force per area $= $ (mass $\cdot$ gravity) / (cross-section area)
$\propto k^3 / k^2 = k$. *(Stress increases linearly with size.)*

A doubled creature has *doubled* bone stress — and bone material
strength is fixed. At some scaling factor, bones break under their
own weight. *(Galileo, *Two New Sciences*, 1638 — the square-cube
law explains why elephants have proportionally thicker legs than
mice.)*
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definitions.**

- **Regular polyhedron:** convex polyhedron with congruent regular
  polygonal faces and the same number of faces meeting at each
  vertex.
- **Schläfli symbol** $\{p, q\}$: regular polyhedron with $p$-gon
  faces, $q$ faces at each vertex.

**The five Platonic solids.**

| Solid | $\{p, q\}$ | $V$ | $E$ | $F$ |
|-------|------------|-----|-----|-----|
| Tetrahedron | $\{3, 3\}$ | 4 | 6 | 4 |
| Cube | $\{4, 3\}$ | 8 | 12 | 6 |
| Octahedron | $\{3, 4\}$ | 6 | 12 | 8 |
| Dodecahedron | $\{5, 3\}$ | 20 | 30 | 12 |
| Icosahedron | $\{3, 5\}$ | 12 | 30 | 20 |

**Theorems (Stereometry §§491–530).**

1. *Euler's formula:* for every convex polyhedron, $V - E + F = 2$.
2. *Five Platonic solids:* exactly five regular polyhedra exist,
   characterised by $\tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}$.
3. *Similarity scaling:* solids similar with linear ratio $k$ have
   surfaces in ratio $k^2$ and volumes in ratio $k^3$.
4. *Square-cube law:* uniform scaling makes mass grow as $k^3$ but
   structural cross-section as $k^2$ — stresses scale as $k$.

:::

A verse:

> *Five solids — Plato saw them, Euclid proved them, no sixth exists.*
> *Tetrahedron, cube, octahedron — and dodecahedron, icosahedron persist.*
> *V minus E plus F is two, for every convex polyhedron known.*
> *Linear k cubes the volume — the law that grows everything you've grown.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Verify Euler's formula for a triangular prism: a 3D shape with
> two triangular bases and three rectangular sides.

$V = 6$ (3 corners on each base), $E = 9$ (3 base edges per
triangle, plus 3 lateral edges) — wait, $E = 3 + 3 + 3 = 9$.
$F = 5$ (2 bases + 3 sides).

$V - E + F = 6 - 9 + 5 = 2$. ✓

**Euler's formula holds.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A cube has edge length $5$. Find its volume and surface
area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = 125$, $S = 6 \cdot 25 = 150$.
</details>

**W2.** Two cubes have edge ratio $1 : 3$. Find the ratio of their
volumes.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 : 27$.
</details>

**W3.** Verify Euler's formula for an octahedron ($V = 6, E = 12,
F = 8$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6 - 12 + 8 = 2$. ✓
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A football (soccer ball) is made of $12$ regular pentagonal
patches and $20$ regular hexagonal patches. Each pentagon shares
its $5$ edges with $5$ hexagons; each hexagon shares $3$ edges with
pentagons and $3$ with other hexagons. Compute $V$, $E$, $F$, and
verify Euler's formula.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$F = 12 + 20 = 32$. Each edge is shared by $2$ faces, so
$E = (12 \cdot 5 + 20 \cdot 6)/2$. Each vertex is shared by $3$
faces (the standard *truncated icosahedron* configuration).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$F = 32$. $E = (60 + 120)/2 = 90$. Vertices: each is shared by 3
faces (1 pentagon + 2 hexagons), and each face contributes its
vertices counted once per shared face:
$V = (12 \cdot 5 + 20 \cdot 6)/3 = 60$.

Check: $V - E + F = 60 - 90 + 32 = 2$. ✓

This is the **truncated icosahedron** — the world's most famous
*Archimedean solid* (and the geometry of fullerene C60).
</details>

**P2.** A regular dodecahedron has edge length $a$. Find the
ratio of its surface area to that of a cube of edge $a$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Dodecahedron: $12$ regular pentagons of side $a$. Pentagon area:
$\dfrac{a^2 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}}{4}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Dodecahedron surface: $12 \cdot \dfrac{a^2 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}}{4}
= 3 a^2 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}$.
Cube surface: $6 a^2$.
Ratio: $\dfrac{3 \sqrt{25 + 10\sqrt 5}}{6} = \dfrac{\sqrt{25 + 10
\sqrt 5}}{2} \approx 3.4377$.
**Dodecahedron has about $3.44$ times the cube's surface for the
same edge.**
</details>

**P3.** A regular tetrahedron and a cube have *the same surface
area*. Find the ratio of their volumes.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set surface areas equal: $\sqrt 3 a_{\text{tet}}^2 = 6
a_{\text{cube}}^2$, where $a_{\text{tet}}$ is tetrahedron edge.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt 3 a_{\text{tet}}^2 = 6 a_{\text{cube}}^2$, so
$a_{\text{tet}}^2 / a_{\text{cube}}^2 = 6 / \sqrt 3 = 2\sqrt 3$.
Volumes: $V_{\text{tet}} = \dfrac{a_{\text{tet}}^3 \sqrt 2}{12}$,
$V_{\text{cube}} = a_{\text{cube}}^3$. Ratio:
$\dfrac{V_{\text{tet}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{12}
\cdot (2\sqrt 3)^{3/2} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{12} \cdot 2 \sqrt{12} \cdot
\sqrt{2\sqrt 3} \approx 0.7264$. **Tetrahedron volume $\approx
72.6\%$ of the cube's** when surface areas match. *(Confirms
intuition: the cube is "more efficient" at enclosing volume per
surface; the sphere is the most efficient.)*
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that the **dual** of each Platonic solid (formed by
placing a vertex at each face centre and connecting vertices of
adjacent faces) is again a Platonic solid:
- Tetrahedron ↔ Tetrahedron (self-dual)
- Cube ↔ Octahedron
- Dodecahedron ↔ Icosahedron

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The dual of a polyhedron with Schläfli symbol $\{p, q\}$ has
symbol $\{q, p\}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
| Original | Schläfli | Dual Schläfli | Dual solid |
|----------|----------|---------------|------------|
| Tetrahedron $\{3,3\}$ | $(3, 3)$ | $(3, 3)$ | **Tetrahedron** (self-dual) |
| Cube $\{4,3\}$ | $(4, 3)$ | $(3, 4)$ | **Octahedron** |
| Dodecahedron $\{5,3\}$ | $(5, 3)$ | $(3, 5)$ | **Icosahedron** |

By Problem 3, $\{q, p\}$ is also a valid Platonic Schläfli symbol
iff $\{p, q\}$ is. So the duals are all Platonic. **QED.**
*(Duality is a deep theme in geometry, group theory, and physics.)*
</details>

**S2.** *Sphere packing.* The densest packing of identical spheres
in 3D is the **face-centred cubic (FCC)** arrangement, which fills
$\dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt 2} \approx 74\%$ of space. Verify this density
for the FCC lattice.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
In the FCC unit cell (a cube of side $a$), there are $4$ spheres
each of diameter $a / \sqrt 2$ (touching across the face diagonal).
Volume of $4$ spheres / volume of cube.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
FCC unit cell: a cube of edge $a$. Spheres at $8$ corners (each
contributing $1/8$) plus $6$ face centres (each $1/2$): total
$8 \cdot 1/8 + 6 \cdot 1/2 = 4$ full spheres per unit cell.

Spheres touch across face diagonals: face diagonal $= 4r$, so
$a \sqrt 2 = 4r$, giving $r = a \sqrt 2 / 4 = a / (2\sqrt 2)$.

Total sphere volume: $4 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{16}{3}
\pi \cdot \dfrac{a^3}{16 \sqrt 2} = \dfrac{\pi a^3}{3 \sqrt 2}$.

Density: $\dfrac{\pi a^3 / (3\sqrt 2)}{a^3} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt 2}
\approx 0.7405$. **Verified.** *(Kepler's conjecture, finally
proved by Hales 1998–2017.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Stereometry*, §§493–530.

**K1.** Show that the **centres** of the six faces of a cube of
side $a$ are the vertices of an **octahedron**, and find that
octahedron's edge length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The face-centre points of a cube are at $(\pm a/2, 0, 0)$,
$(0, \pm a/2, 0)$, $(0, 0, \pm a/2)$ — six points symmetric about
the cube's centre. Each pair of "adjacent" face centres is at
distance...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The six face centres are at $(\pm a/2, 0, 0)$, $(0, \pm a/2, 0)$,
$(0, 0, \pm a/2)$ relative to the cube's centre. Adjacent face
centres (e.g. $(a/2, 0, 0)$ and $(0, a/2, 0)$) are at distance
$\sqrt{(a/2)^2 + (a/2)^2} = a / \sqrt 2$. The six points form a
regular octahedron of edge $a / \sqrt 2 \approx 0.707 a$.
**Octahedron edge $= a / \sqrt 2$.** *(Stereometry §495 — *cube
and octahedron are duals*.)*
</details>

**K2.** A regular tetrahedron of edge $a$ is inscribed in a cube
of edge $a' = a / \sqrt 2$. *(I.e., the tetrahedron's vertices are
4 of the cube's 8 vertices, no two adjacent.)* Verify this and
find the volume ratio.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
4 cube vertices: $(0,0,0), (a',a',0), (a',0,a'), (0,a',a')$ — check
they form a regular tetrahedron of edge $a' \sqrt 2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Pairwise distances: $\sqrt{(a')^2 + (a')^2} = a' \sqrt 2$. So the
inscribed tetrahedron has edge $a' \sqrt 2$. With $a = a' \sqrt 2$,
$a' = a / \sqrt 2$ — confirmed.

Volume ratio: $V_{\text{tet}} = \dfrac{a^3 \sqrt 2}{12}$,
$V_{\text{cube}} = (a')^3 = \dfrac{a^3}{2\sqrt 2}$.
Ratio $V_{\text{tet}} / V_{\text{cube}} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2 / 12}
{1 / (2\sqrt 2)} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2 \cdot 2\sqrt 2}{12} = \dfrac{4}{12}
= \dfrac{1}{3}$. **The inscribed tetrahedron is $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of
the cube's volume.** *(Stereometry §496 exercise.)*
</details>

**K3.** Show that the area of an equilateral triangle of side $s$
is $\dfrac{s^2 \sqrt 3}{4}$, and use it to derive the volume of a
regular tetrahedron of side $s$ as $\dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Equilateral triangle area: drop the altitude, use Pythagoras.
Tetrahedron volume: $\tfrac{1}{3}$ base × height; height $=
s \sqrt{2/3}$ (Ch 1 S2 in this tier).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Equilateral triangle area: altitude $h = s \sqrt 3 / 2$. Area
$= \tfrac{1}{2} s \cdot s\sqrt 3 / 2 = \dfrac{s^2 \sqrt 3}{4}$.

Tetrahedron volume: $V = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{s^2 \sqrt 3}{4}
\cdot s\sqrt{2/3} = \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 3 \cdot \sqrt 2}{12 \sqrt 3}
= \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §498.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book II — Stereometry*, §§491–530 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/%5BKiselev,Givental%5DKiselev's_Geom2.pdf).
The five-Platonic-solids classification (Problem 3) is Kiselev's
§493 — directly following Euclid Book XIII.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Euler's formula is $V + E + F = 2$."** It is *minus* in the
   middle: $V - E + F = 2$. The minus is essential — the
   alternating sum is what makes the topology come out right
   (it generalises to the *Euler characteristic*).
2. **"There are six Platonic solids."** No — there are **exactly
   five** in 3D. *(Six in 4D, three in dimensions $\ge 5$ — but
   that's a different topic.)*
3. **"Volume scales by $k^2$ for similar solids."** No — by **$k^3$**.
   Surface area scales by $k^2$; volume by $k^3$. Mixing them up is
   the most common error in this area.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you prove the *5-Platonic-solids* theorem (Problem 3) on a
   fresh sheet, deriving the angle inequality
   $\tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}$?
2. Did *Euler's formula* (Problem 2) feel like a planar-graph
   argument once stretched flat?
3. Can you state the three scaling laws — linear $k$, area $k^2$,
   volume $k^3$ — and explain the *square-cube law* (Problem 7)?

- **3 yes:** **You have completed Kiselev *Stereometry* in
  Russian-classical style.** The four-tier Kiselev geometry track
  is now in place.
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 3 — the Platonic classification is the
  chapter's centrepiece.
- **0–1 yes:** the trio Problems 2, 3, 4 form one big idea (Euler
  + classification + scaling); replay them.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg2.polyhedra`](../02-polyhedra/index.qmd) — vertex / edge /
  face vocabulary; volume formulas for prisms and pyramids.
- [`kg2.sphere`](../04-sphere/index.qmd) — the sphere as the
  $n \to \infty$ limit of a regular $n$-hedron, and the comparison
  point for shape-efficiency arguments.

### What this unlocks

- [`t6.groups`](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/01-groups/index.qmd) —
  the symmetries of each Platonic solid form a *finite group*
  (the *icosahedral group* of order $120$ is one of the great
  finite simple groups).
- [`t7.langlands-program`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/15-langlands-program/index.qmd) —
  the dihedral, tetrahedral, octahedral, icosahedral groups are
  the simplest *Galois representations*.
- [`t6.spectral-graph-theory`](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/14-spectral-graph-theory/index.qmd) —
  Euler's formula generalises to the *Euler characteristic* of any
  surface, the gateway to topology.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book II — Chapter 5: Regular polyhedra and similarity
  (§§491–530).** Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/%5BKiselev,Givental%5DKiselev's_Geom2.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)
  (computational mensuration);
  [`cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/12-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)
  (combined solids).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development
  of *Cauchy's rigidity theorem* (every convex polyhedron is rigid)
  in the appendix.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Game engines render Platonic solids as
*primitives* — the icosahedron is the standard *low-poly sphere*
because it has the highest face-symmetry-to-face-count ratio. Dual
relationships (cube↔octahedron) are exploited in *normal-map
encoding* and *cubemap projection*.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** **Graph neural networks** generalise the Euler-formula
intuition: structure flows along edges, with vertex-edge-face
incidences encoded as adjacency matrices. The Platonic solids are
the canonical small *vertex-transitive* graphs — natural test
cases for symmetry-aware architectures.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** **Cauchy's rigidity theorem** (1813): every convex
polyhedron is rigid — its shape is determined by its face shapes
and combinatorial structure. This was the first major theorem in
*combinatorial geometry* and is still the gateway to questions in
mechanism theory and protein folding.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Plato (*Timaeus* — the cosmological role of the
  five solids); Euclid (*Elements* Book XIII — first complete
  classification proof); Cauchy (*Mémoire sur les polygones et les
  polyèdres* — the rigidity theorem and the polyhedron-flatten
  proof of Euler).
- **The argument $\tfrac{1}{p} + \tfrac{1}{q} > \tfrac{1}{2}$**
  (Problem 3) is one of the cleanest classifications in mathematics
  — three lines, total certainty. Worth memorising.
- **What this lesson does not do:** classify the Archimedean
  solids (semi-regular: same vertex configuration, but different
  face polygons). There are 13 of them; that's a separate topic
  worth its own lesson.
- **Kiselev *Stereometry* — track complete.** Five chapters, all
  aligned to Givental's English translation. Together with
  Planimetry's nine chapters, the Kiselev geometry track is in
  place from definitions to the Platonic classification.
:::
